title: Voice Inversion Scrambling
short description: Analog Voice Inversion Scrambling divides the spectra into multiple bands and swaps them.
description:
VIS, or **Voice inversion scrambling**, is a property of a NFM signal that allows for scrambled transmissions to take place. 

It obscures the content of a transmission. It is sometimes used in public service radio, automobile racing, cordless telephones and the Family Radio Service. Without a descrambler, the transmission makes the speaker "sound like Donald Duck", or a false LSB or USB signal that had been detuned slightly, depending on scrambling parameters set forth by the scrambler hardware. . It is called "voice inversion", but the technique operates on the passband of the information and so can be applied to any information being transmitted, thus leading to the "false" LSB/USB detuned signals.

Since analog NFM in the US is slowly dying out (with the exception of a couple of systems) and fewer systems implemented this.
